results.description: DESCRIPTION:THE GOAL OF THE PROJECT IS TO RESEARCH AND BRING ENVIRONMENTAL JUSTICE-FOCUSED STRATEGIES TO MITIGATE SEPTIC FAILURES IN COASTAL COMMUNITIES UNDER THE ENVIRONMENTAL CONDITIONS OF RISING SEA LEVELS AND INCREASED FLOODING.ACTIVITIES:RESEARCHERS WILL 1) IDENTIFY AND PARTNER WITH VULNERABLE UNDERSERVED COMMUNITIES THAT ARE AT RISK OF SEPTIC SYSTEM FAILURES NOW AND IN THE FUTURE DUE TO CLIMATE CHANGES; 2) ADDRESS THE ANTECEDENT CONDITIONS (INCLUDING HISTORICAL SETTLEMENT PATTERNS AND EXCLUSION FROM MUNICIPAL SEWERS) THAT POTENTIALLY EXACERBATE CONTAMINANT EXPOSURE BY COLLECTING PRIMARY DATA ON SEPTIC HEALTH AND THE ENVIRONMENT TO DEVELOP COMPOSITE RISKS; AND 3) EVALUATE WHERE AND HOW SPECIFIC POLICY AND TECHNOLOGY SOLUTIONS CAN ADDRESS THE COMPOUNDED RISKS, WHILE ENSURING THAT BENEFITS ACCRUE IN A WAY THAT ADDRESSES PAST DISCRIMINATORY PRACTICES.SUBRECIPIENT:RESOURCES FOR THE FUTURE ($477,000) WILL MEASURE THE VALUE OF SEWER AS CAPTURED IN PROPERTY VALUES AND AS MEASURED BY THE PREFERENCES OF LOCAL RESIDENTS AND COMMUNITY LEADERS. GEORGE MASON UNIVERSITY ($226,000) WILL BE RESPONSIBLE FOR THE FLOOD HAZARDS MODELING COMPONENT OF THE PROPOSED WORK, INCLUDING CLIMATE INDUCED COASTAL FLOODING AND SEA-LEVEL RISE INUNDATION. SOUTHEAST RURAL COMMUNITY ASSISTANCE PROJECT ($117,705) WILL CO-PRODUCE RESEARCH (E.G., GUIDING RESEARCH QUESTIONS TO ENSURE THEY ARE RELEVANT); SUPPORT DATA-SHARING WHERE APPROPRIATE; ADMINISTER A SURVEY OR SUPPORT RESIDENT INTERVIEWS; SUPPORT COLLECTION OF WATER/SOIL SAMPLES; AND SUPPORT RESEARCH OUTREACH.OUTCOMES:DELIVERABLES INCLUDE ANNUAL AND FINAL RESEARCH REPORTS AS WELL AS SCIENTIFIC PUBLICATIONS, EDUCATIONAL INFORMATION, AND SOLUTIONS FOR PUBLIC HEALTH AGENCIES AND COMMUNITY PLANNERS TO PREVENT AND MITIGATE SEPTIC FAILURES IN UNDERSERVED COASTAL COMMUNITIES. EXPECTED OUTCOMES ARE PREVENTION OF WATER CONTAMINATION FROM SEPTIC FAILURES AND IMPROVED PUBLIC HEALTH. INTENDED BENEFICIARIES INCLUDE UNDERSERVED COASTAL COMMUNITIES IN EASTERN MARYLAND, STATE AND LOCAL WATER RESOURCE MANAGERS, AND STATE AND LOCAL PUBLIC HEALTH AGENCIES.
